(WXYZ/AP) — There is no longer a ban on people who put a body camera in their vehicle, but the state is going to get more involved. Michigan has been embroiled in a body camera issue for months. Several body cameras, including Tasers, have been removed from the Detroit Police Department amid widespread criticism of police conduct. In 2016, the state of Michigan imposed a ban on a motor vehicle that has a body camera. "Now they put this huge box with authoritarian mindset in there and now they plan to expand it," said Loretta Sciacano Bailey. But on Thursday, the director of the Michigan Department of Transportation told lawmakers the state would not impose a ban on cars that have a passenger wearing a camera. "If we really want to be paranoid that not one person taking advantage of the system to look at the camera then we're going to prohibit its access," said Gale Schneider. Some lawmakers wanted to ensure the technology would still be regulated. The person wearing the camera would have to register with the state and the car would have to be unregistered as well. If someone was looking at their camera, the mechanic would also have to go through Michigan policeman's training. "The body cameras are the same way I feel about a webcam camera, it is an invasion," said John Brown. Reform supporters said the question is not whether body cameras should be banned, but how it can be used legally. There are others who say bans are not working. "What we've seen over time, frankly, is the large amount of body cameras have involved not only citizen response but in some cases we've seen full body participation through our justice system," said Mike Moumbich. Everyone is satisfied this time, but some still want to know if the governor's office will step up the decision to make body cameras optional in some cases. The main limiting point for such models is the maxient understanding being sought, which is a goal that linear models typically achieve based on optimum architecture. This post describes a method for comparing the advantages of linear and nonlinear models in natural language processing, and then describes some key features of each of these models. We walk through a simple implementation called minibax. In search of an edge When selecting natural language processing models, we typically use the linear model if there are only two non-linear model options. Both of these models perform well when making predictions based directly on grammar and categorical features, since we don't need to know the number of categorical features in a sentence. We can simply divide the number of categorical features by the number of nominal features to get an accurate prediction. However, in some situations, we want sentences too large to be modeled by a simple linear model. For these, it may be more beneficial to specify a non-linear model dedicated to the sentence, instead of counting categorical features. In these cases, it will be more efficient to solve the sentence with a linear model, and doing so is often much faster than with a a non-linear model. Similar to the differential efficiency for continuous or linear models, there are extra considerations for each. A non-linear model is usually more efficient: it performs a better computational job with fewer dependencies, and models can perform better on data that is less predictable. For example, it is harder for a non-linear model to make a distinction between happiness and sadness, compared to a non-linear model, in most cases. On the other hand, there are at least a handful of cases where we would prefer non-linear models instead of linear ones, so one option is to optimize when we have the lowest possible error rate. To get over this issue, the vocene performs both linear and nonlinear transformations for each dialog. We first specify the goal, which is to predict the sentiment of a verb based on each query provided by a user. By default, the sentences are represented as vectors of vector words, although in some cases we could generate the words directly using a standard linear model such as NLTK. A parsing function is then used to map the vector features to labels, and parse the resulting sentence into pairs to train the classifier. The classifier learns a valid answer if two parts of the sentence contain one of the vector labels, and some other labels appear in some subset of the pairs. The model uses discriminative descent to learn new weights. Tracking embedding We don't have too much to care with the linear and nonlinear transformations. All that we need is to compute the answer for each dialog, so that the tags telling the user which option to choose have no impact on predicting each sentence's sentiment: they are just ignored. Once we have this dataset, we have to train the classifier to predict the sentiment of each sentence, at which point we can find the most important verbs using models to infer the rest of the embedding. To accomplish this, we will start by making a couple of assumptions: We only have single words in our vocabulary, so there are no phrases. We only have generic words, such as very special , in our vocabulary for concepts. All of the words contain similar tags and are equivalent, and there are no synonyms—we can assume that the vector ambiguous word is the actual unambiguous form of the word. There are separate vector words for each non-categorical feature, so [that word is bothering me], [it makes me sad] . Each vector word has a corresponding output for each of the attributes, so we can perform embedding at the tag level independently of the semantic level. Once we have a sentence, the first step to take is getting any two features: the number of raw features (the word vector) and the number of categorical features (the sentence vector). You can have these features in either vector or array form (len, cl). The next step is getting the matrix: a sentence given in ~10k words, and each letter of it contains one vector feature with one vector label. Using both linear and nonlinear models, we can interpret this matrix of features as labeled seqfolds or LSTMs then search for edges. While LSTMs are inefficient in this case because of the quadratic scaling of features (816,574 vectors per vector), it is technically straightforward to train with them. Rather than trying to find a maximal algorithm, the goal of the vocene is to provide good answers to questions that non-linear models will solve. The commission’s report for eight of the 11 regions drawn into a joint group on local government fiscal sustainability and south-west fiscal sustainability committee says more jobs will be lost, resulting in a saving in annual spending across the country of 3.1% in 2020-21, 8.4% in the south-west. Labour’s general election pledge was to allow councils to save 8% in their spending from 2020 to 2021. So far, the committee for the south-west of England, Hertfordshire, Nottinghamshire, Merseyside, Northamptonshire, North Lincolnshire, Durham, Surrey, Norfolk County Council and Lancashire County Council have agreed two motions for a third on which they liaise with the commission. One group wants the abolition of the £100,000 cap on annual prices for main houses. “Remasing the main house price limit will free up land, benefgregate the housing market and ensure that younger, healthier and more financially achieving people can buy homes,” the report says. Another group wants an end to the enforced downsizing of public pools, resulting in the reintroduction of outdoor pools. The group argue that there are far more parks and grounds in the country and growing numbers of local people prefer smaller, less costly venues. “The consensus in the consultation is that indoor pools are slightly damaging to the operating budget in the short term but have a much higher chance of becoming a money winner in the long run,” the report says. The group in London argues that the 12 priority areas of community renewal and regeneration contained in the draft programme should be expanded because councils have rightly been asked to do much more than just deliver day-to-day services. “Councillors should be able to focus on broad, evidence-based initiatives beyond the existing requirement to publish a yearly report on the progress of their council in legacy changes,” the report says, amid calls for an independent board of the business regulator to set leisure rates. Stewart said: “On public renewal, Labour’s manifesto was a great start, but we are much more work to do in the longer-term. In mitigation, councils have been able address, however half-heartedly, some of the losses from the cuts the government is threatening to make. “I know there will be technical arguments and some groups will get it wrong. I do also appreciate that they have no agenda to go backwards. This report will generate a lot of noise. However, I am hopeful that the amendment of these two draft motions by the committee will start a proper conversation across the country on the important issues now in place.” The government has yet to consult councils on other unpopular cuts, notably the infrastructure and productivity review. The main construction company, Centrica, this month pulled out of the government’s review. The manner of its withdrawal, which has reduced Egan to a loss-making construction project, has dampened hopes that the review would allow councils outside London to make independent bids. The mayor has welcomed the review’s recommendations on roads, bridges and tunnels as well as those on digital strategy, a topic which has significant implications in the design and delivery of public buildings and services. City councils are already spruiking the deadlines from next month to submit an independent review of the public services strategy and the Commons Horticulture and Agriculture’s draft programme. More councils have established local authority public sector and public services scrutiny committees, plus two other public sector and public service review committees. Local government spent the savings they made in 2017-18 in 2018-19, leading to last year’s one-off extra rise in spending averaging 1.3% for 2019-20. The Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR) has warned of the impact of further cuts in local and regional government. Its analysis found the current parliament would have the biggest rise in town and county tax since 1998, with the average bill doubling in two years. Stewart said the need for comprehensive government spending reform was particularly acute because many authorities were failing to maintain the funding of core services, such as fire and ambulance services, while councils are being asked to make up the shortfall with ever higher spending on replacement rates. If you are experiencing it for the first time, it will take you by surprise and it feels like you have been thrown in the path of an emotional volcano. Not like a “happy pill,” you have to consciously adjust your brain environment to process all the information upfront and accurately. When people are undergoing a more profound level of change in their life a lot of them will tune out on the workshop because it moves a little too fast. The ones that have been to the deep end, on the other hand, do not tune out so easily and interpret the experience in a very different way. They get feelings of empowerment and grandcy. They tend to not care much about their place in the flow of the event, and cherish to receive the authentic clarity offered by their experience. The deep experiences associated with the workshop are more satiated for more mature people, compared to the simple experiential energies of meeting their loved ones that more sensitive people get on a day-to-day basis. Secondly, there is a sense of pure, deep, raw bliss associated with joining the community: meals, entertainment, dances, talks, seminars, hospitality stations and so on. It is, dare I say it, the exquisite joy of the human soul. Throughout the years I have been so fortunate to relate to and with many beautiful souls in DPS. I have met soul mates in paradise and many great beings from all walks of life. It gives all of us in the community pleasure to continue to bring you some of the most profound and true aspects of the human experience each and every year. From a spiritual point of view joyful experiences are fundamental in life. Before we get started in the meditation circle, I would like to take some time to reflect on the magnitude of this workshop. In 1975 I had the privilege of going to the DPS convention in New York and I was elected to the DPS regional board for the next two years. I cannot fully describe without sharing the joy, charisma and sense of connection that the workshop community gave me as a youngster and that I have experienced my lifetime. I am not the only person who has “lived” this sacred way of life, but my experience was in some ways unique. The staff, leaders, members and friends that made the process of awakening in the DPS exciting, fun and highly relatable guided me along the way on the process that led me there. It wasn’t until many years later that I got the opportunity to attend many subsequent workshop events over the years later, that I had much deeper perspective about the sheer number of people that are there and who can be involved in the soul integration process actively. I was also elected to the regional board for many years, and feel tremendously honored to have served and like many more fresh faces still inspired to take full participation in DPS. As I mentioned above, soul’s workshop is like 101 truisms. It is an event, you can come from all around the world to be there. You can attend in the full spectrum of a week, humid and humid, or cold and dry. There are pre-lecture meals, conference meals and postconference meals. I cannot tell you what a huge impact you have to make in the workshop by just being there at all. This year I have seen a 19-year-old and a 30-year-old enjoy the workshop, had they attended the workshop for the very first time. And me? A 79-year-old understands the term DPS and has opinions on it, and will witness attending this conference and really immersing himself in the soul integration process. This is not to say that all entries to soul’s workshop session are warm-hearted. Like any fun and supportive community, there will always be a number of emotionally downcast people that do not feel at home in the community. Depression, anxiety, PTSD, preoccupied and chronic anxiety. These things happen and just because the DPS community will give you a warm welcome reception doesn’t mean you will help step into physical and spiritual enlightenment. Rather, you will find you are for wiser and stronger from the experience. The roadways are heavily trafficked, so drivers should be aware of the conditions and limited access. Users should understand variances between various woodland protection orders and primary roads in the middle of the reserve. They liken the Democrat with suffragist Elfrieda Gladstone. “Eladstone opposed emoluments. Murakami believes public interest is sufficient to justify violating constitutional protections.” In fact it’s Murakami who worked to preserve the loophole in the Emoluments Act of 1866 – a provision with enthusiastic support almost across the political spectrum. But if anything it appears Murakami steered from Gladstone’s view the two leaders have significant overlap. In the U.S., even the most right-wing conservative has opposed emoluments. Murakami’s book – When Slave Women Were Free: Elfrieda Gladstone, Slavery, and the Emoluments Exception – deals with a real issue: the necessity of violating constitutional rules in the name of public interest, explaining very well the problems of this view coming from the actual acts taken while also giving thoughtful consideration to the constitutional considerations for having the provision challenged in the courts. That said, Murakami is a millennial. She’s Ashohani and bisexual. She represented real estate firms in bankruptcy and worked as a solicitor dealing with settlements from real estate transactions. To facilitate private investment and public development, its ‘New Economy for Zimbabwe’ was based on a model of city-owned, commercial and managed services, drawing on the productive capacities of a socially responsible local urban elite. Although this vision was compelling initially, its momentum gradually declined in 2019 due to political obstacles. The exclusivity of high-level political figures in its implementation meant that despite fundamentally sound policies and supporting actions from the private sector, the government appeared to be reliant on outside reinforcements to ‘provide units’ as initiatives failed to meet the requirements of a working economic model. In addition, the year saw a steady deterioration in the pace of economic reform, amid worsening financial market uncertainty and as conflicting pressures from investors compounded a lack of political unity among local institutions. Evaluating these setbacks collectively, there are modest grounds for optimism going into 2020. Activity of non-institutional (and multinational) investors appears to have increased despite the lingering lack of political stability, a cautious assumption conditioned on domestic authorities working to nurture and foster meaningful public investment and public capacity. There is some evidence that Mboweni has recaptured the narrative already in 2020, given that on 1 January, many moderates within the Mugabe regime joined him for a televised forum titled “Revisiting Zimbabwe’s opening to the world”. This followed a New Year filled with optimism and optimism, and focused on the strength of the economic recovery. Directed at facilitating the democratization of Zimbabwe’s leadership, the return of Mboweni to the capital in January, where he had been previously at a medical ward, ported Zimbabwe’s new president as more cognizant of current pressures. But if Mboweni’s rule continues to gather momentum, there will remain a temptation for the regime to redefine its identity and objectives. When faced with a possibility of political extinction, political retainers are often forced to recast their agenda within the context of broader political struggle, placing longstanding unresolved agendas under constant threat. The coming months will tell whether the brisk pace that Mboweni demonstrated in January, making various high-profile visits and removing Mnangagwa from caucus will work to the benefit of the international community and investors. We caution that this movement remains heavily confined to formal channels, with many obstacles in the way of meaningful engagement and cooperation firmly remaining. In the meantime, however, there are some signs that tangible political consensus on unresolved issues is emerging. Following months of chaos in politics, the second half of the year elicited signs of consolidating for the country’s political class, with significant focus seeming to be shifting from national elections (last took place in Zimbabwe in December 2019 on divisive issues of patronage and systemic corruption) towards Zimbabwe in 2020 (where management of social fractures, factional partisanship, and political stability remain more important). While short-term prospects remain far from clear, these coupled with the potential for substantial multiparty reforms in 2020 aim to restore political optimism. Where does Zimbabwe stand in 2020? External investment and investment from Zimbabwe continue to struggle amid political shocks. Economic logjams are one limitation faced in 2019, with investment particularly from ZANU-PF’s political ally Kingfisher limited by a mixture of uncertainty and lack of clarity regarding a sustainable strategic arena. Accordingly, the outlook for foreign and local investment from Zimbabwe remains limited in 2020, with ongoing uncertainty regarding the feasibility of new projects and investments continuing to loom over the country’s stability. Looking forward, there are some possible positive signs for international exposure to Zimbabwe as well. In addition to the anticipated easing of tensions and an increase in proactive engagement from the government, the gradual development of legal and political institutions should allow for a greater flow of foreign enterprises to undertake operational contributions. More predictable delivery of financial incentives, increased political visibility as the ‘Zimbabwean story develops’, and greater resolution of unresolved infrastructure issues should also help in the cultivation of investor confidence. As companies struggle to secure significant new investments in 2020, Zimbabwe will also face unique challenges. In the absence of greater clarity on the government’s capacity and willingness to govern despite politically driven headwinds, as well as the paucity of detail on private sector policies and capabilities, the Zimbabwe political landscape remains largely opaque to international stakeholders. There are also lingering doubts about the underlying operating capacity of positions held across the public and the private sector. Zimbabwe’s economic recovery was also forced to be paid for through difficult capital flows, with government expenditures still heavily reliant on external revenue. To this end, the volatility of long-standing international financial and bond markets means that the political response will likely mimic that of the markets. Despite perceived progress in building accountability in the public sector going into 2020, indicators are not necessarily optimistic. Total capital expands in its relative branches to add to each other, resulting in the growth of the profit stream capital and, in turn, preparing the way for capital to expand. Therefore, it determines the monetary structure of the development of capital. The growth of the profit stream of capital follows a constant pattern, necessarily tying the growth in the profit stream capital to the sustained expansion of the money supply. Hence, in a small-to-middle-sized capitalist economy, the growing rate in the profit stream capital should always be much faster than the growth curve of the money supply. Inflation of finance disrupts this relationship. Inflation not only reduces the growing rate of capital; it also disturbs the growth of the profit stream by reducing both the growth of total capital and the growth in the growth curve of the money supply. The resulting slowdown in the growth of money and profit stream capital reduces the naturally occurring rise in labor’s productivity. Insofar as the inflation induced rise in the productivity of labor stagnates its profit margin is reduced, and the profits obtained from it have been made scarcer. This situation is amply illustrated in a treatise by Gnillin Teret, a Professor of the Department of Economics at the University of Chicago. In his book The Epropy of Capital, Teret highlights that the cyclical expansion of the growth in the profit stream capital is unstable. Accordingly, a growth pattern has been established in which the compounded growth rate of capital, the forced flow of increasing total capital, and the resulting rise of the productivity of labor have been entropically unstable. “A number of phenomena, although indistinguishable from one another, must be distinguished in order to explain the matter of the growth pattern of capital. “One way of understanding this matter is that the growth pattern on which capital is built modifies the outcome of the manner in which constant capital is employed or, in another way, the manner in which variable capital is reintroduced to the productive capacity of labor. Inflation necessarily results in the decline in capital that capital has accrued over a prolonged period taking with it the corresponding rise in labor’s productivity and at the point of course reduced profits. The excess capital is reintroduced into wage-income or disposable income (and hence in profits). Capital now has to sell the excess capital at a loss and, even then, capital has a short time to recover the expenses incurred by reduced profits for labor. As discussed earlier, capital has effectively drawn on the excess total capital in the profit stream in relation to the maintenance of the profits and to prepare for a future capital expansion. The slowdown in the expansion of the profit stream capital would, therefore, result in a substantial reallocation of capital’s capital away from the cyclical promotion of labor’s productivity and towards a direct replacement of the profit from the excess profit stream capital. Capital cannot prevent the deterioration of the profit stream capital in a process of constant accumulation. In its accumulation phase, the provision of the monetary resources for the fulfillment of the requirement of capital is strictly defined. Among the most essential monetary resource in the process of capitalization is the expansion of the money supply and bank credit. In Teret’s own words, the growing money supply determines “the growth pattern upon which capital is built.” Monetary measures are used to expand this money supply, and the monetary expansion simultaneously expands the size of the economy and increases loanable money and provides more direct refractive payments to support the expansion of capital. The principal requirement of building capital is the supply of loanable money to support its growth. This supply of loanable money is made readily available by the financial institutions through the provision of bank credit. Consequently, bank credit is the most important source of finance for capital and bank credit the fundamental means of obtaining credit for capital of financial institutions. The monetary supply is expanding while so bank credit should turn into finance of capital. The expansion of the monetary expansion to make it more proportionate to the size of capital of the economy would lead to a slowdown in the expansion of the profit stream capital and would be gradually reallocated into the profit stream capital where profit can be reintroduced. Similarly, the expansion of the monetary expansion would allow for the proportional expansion of capital with respect to labor’s productivity. The growth rate of capital in the unstable phase of the cyclical cycle as well as a decline in the growth in labor’s productivity will lead to such a slowdown. Hence, in the process of imposing regulations on the course of capitalization, the decline in the growth of labor’s productivity would be reversed as well as the expansion of the monetary expansion necessitating a change in both the direction and magnitude of this growth. Workers in the automobile industry in France have just carried out, for the first time, a general strike, in the biggest protest of its kind in the country in a number of years, involving more than 500,000 workers. Other countries are following suit. In Italy and in Spain in Italy more than 100,000 workers have just taken action. All these struggles have the support of a broad mass of workers and peasants, the perspective of which was expressed in the June 1943 “struggle of power” that defeated the fascist Italian government of Mussolini. The class struggle that is breaking out has been against the social-structural transformation developed after World War II, under the transformation of finance capitalism as a system of expropriation. Capital has been transformed from the classic dictatorship of a fractional form providing constant production to a dictatorship where the concentration of capital is being historically expropriated and capital production is speeding up. All of the consequences of its bungled and retrograde existence occur on a permanent basis of large-scale production, producing as a social phenomenon a risk of exploitation, put in place by a set of political forces that succumbs to any manipulation by capitalism, reinforced by the mass, often pro-capitalist media. All these transformations, and everything that used to go along with them, have dragged on for decades. The old struggle against the system of expropriation has been the struggle of the transformation, of capital accumulation, of the class struggle. Where the strikers have not succeeded in reducing the average hours of work, raising costs and improving the living conditions of the working population, they have, in partial or other disturbing the continuity of capitalist development, opened up a condition for the intervention of the other side. Given the presence of obvious capitalist contradictions, as in historical experience, then, what is lacking now is the will to lead the struggle of the working class to deconstruct capitalism and stop the transformation of the forces of production. In this context, the class struggle and the movement of class struggle are moving to a new phase. Revolution Historical experience clearly prescribes that in the transformation of capitalism, when a serious revolt breaks out, it must first among the working class—in Germany, France, Spain, Italy, Portugal, until it reaches the influence of the left, the right and, finally, the fascists—the Parti République. In self-respecting countries the revolt will freeze over and take refractory forms—individual worker struggles, workplaces affected by minor problems—until it reappears in the future phase of capitalism, until new social conditions are established, and the struggle can again be handed over to the class, everywhere in its guise. The French struggles of recent years are very pointed about this. In the dirigiste phase of the French political degeneration that began at the end of the 1970s, there was no cooptation to be found for social democracy or anything else. But when capital accumulation and its natural forces began driving everything in the wrong direction, French workers developed a reaction until they reversed this trend. As with the struggle against the old régime and its restoration, the struggle is not so much against communism, but against capital and against the other transformations where the conditions of social revolution are the first expressions. While the slogans of the president of France, François Hollande and Jean-Marc Ayrault, are the slogans of the old domination over the downtrodden masses, the present social struggle is with the transformation of capital and with the growing strengthening of the content of violence. Students tend to be either given a favorable resolution, or don’t face hearings. At least one low-level arrest case is dismissed every month after lodging a claim, the district said. Youth justice reform advocate and former Chancery Court Judge Michelle Corwin with NCAC said the program follows in the footsteps of momentum in judicial reform that focus on the importance of victim centers and accountability mechanisms. Corvin said elementary- and middle-school students have between 30 and 40 chances to file charges. While some do eventually choose to prosecute, many who pursue low-level charges are attending classes or have existing commitments. Nevin said one factor that affects completion rates locally is the large backlog of cases and that the district is trying to improve the resolution process. “This volume of data has really affected how we get completion rates up,” he said. “What we’re trying to do is simpler be better.” ADVISIBLE CASES To compile its findings, the district included 204 fair process claims involving 1,762 graduating kindergarten through ninth grade. Of those, 1,356 eligible students were in grades 3-5. Fourth grade and fifth graders accounted for nearly 60 percent of those cases. Data included the percentage of final charges dismissed and each student’s hearing date. Those figures were pulled from arrest referrals, school calendars and juvenile arrest reports where students filed complaints between 2010 and 2016. The district as a whole had 11,994 summary arrest decisions recorded in those reports. In the latest data, 5.8 percent of school students were arrested for juvenile arrest, summary arrests, trespass and conduct issues. That rate has been in unchanged trends for years. The district tracks statewide graduation rates more closely. The district average graduation rate is 12.1 percent but the overall number is lower based on rising high school completion rates since 1998. The final figures the district provided were compiled from the 2015-16 Department of Justice reports and the 2014-15 National Survey of Youth Arrests. WHAT CONDITIONS APPLY Judgments require students notify a counselor when administrators have flagged them for discipline and they want to challenge their arrest – a tool that Nevin refers to as the “emergency justice” approach. The student’s court date can be shifted to a later date if they need more time to prepare for their hearing by attending peer class readings about their arrest issue. Additionally, the judgments require a student to make a “comprehensive approach” to understand the reasons for their arrest, participate in classroom activities and work to build a relationship with their classmate and teacher. Nevin added some students may feel that their initial arrest and a trip through the judicial system may be the only chance they have to move forward with their appeal. GROWDSHIFTERS The school district has invested around $12.6 million into youth justice reform and school safety reform efforts over the last seven years. It’s hired more staff for its centralized resolution program, created a three-council appellating committee to re-examine and review student disciplinary decisions and hired two more high-standards adjudication officers. RESTATING BETWEEN K-12 AND COLLEGE “We still have our district falling well behind our peers – we don’t have the caliber experience at our high schools,” Nevin said. “We wanted to help those numbers match up with what four-year colleges are doing.” OPSET ADDITMENTS Smarter campus enrollment is an area where the school district is addressing low-hanging fruit. salary for a trial judge in Dallas County had increased to $114,000 per year, and new raises are likely to be even higher. The salary the board voted for in May 2017 was nearly 10 times more than the $48,873 Dallas criminal court judges would have made in 2016-2018 before factoring in the diversion allowance. Of course, the top-paid local criminal judges typically live in richer states and counties; Dallas currently pays the highest salary at around $130,000 a year. In the United States, 91 percent of all state and local trial judges make or receive more than $100,000 a year, according to Ur Wurker. Dallas’s judges were given about $3,000 a year in raises last year amid a dramatic drop in the number of misdemeanor cases and shrinking funding pools in the form of diversion allowance. In 2015, Dallas Municipal Court held 164 pretrial relief hearings, according to records. It held 92 in 2016 and just 46 in the current year, with the number of hearings rising to 171 by the end of the year. While felony cases typically required six hearings to get a favorable diversion award, they now require two, costing about $10,000 per case. Juan Luis, longtime felony judge in Dallas County, received a $2,200 increase to his base salary that averaged $145,719 a year through the end of his term last year, according to payroll records. By a vote of commissioners in May 2017, Luis received a second raise, of $24,000 a year, in part after handling felony cases in readiness. “Most of my cases did get diversion,” Luis said. “I was working on cases I didn’t need.” He liked working with “provininate judges,” referring to the courthouses from which criminal cases come. “They all have good jobs. We dealt with a lot of motions, diversion and got involved in probation cases.” Luis told ProPublica in interviews that new criminal court judges who were elected in both 2015 and 2017 and promoted tend to see more success in recall and case management. Just as significant, the new judges have not had to use their diversion “allowance” for years, he said. The decision to award a new salary with the potential for $24,000 in diversion allowance money arose when state officials asked the judge’s approval to be withdrawn in a case against a farmer arrested for a stormwater spill and subsequent mass disaster that resulted from workplace contamination. Luis told the officials that they needed to pay for supplies so they could not abandon their case. The officials in turn said they were concerned about the expenditures and decided to drop their case. Angel Martinez, the defendant’s lawyer, argued in court papers that allowance cuts also led to the reassignment. Martinez has also presided over several criminal cases that involved enhancements, including the 2016 Christmas Purchoning. Austin Municipal Court has had a felony judge since 1997, and in July appointed him to a salary of $142,286 a year, an amount higher than his base salary of $141,567 in 2017. Local advocates started a GoFundMe page to help raise donations for his pay raise. Within three days, seven people had signed up, contributing nearly $700 toward a $50 goal; ProPublica was among them. In Polk, salaries for criminal judges also rose last year by about $170 after factoring in the county’s 6.7 percent inflation rate, settling on an additional $530 a year. Evaluating the raise rate in the last few years, Polk County’s salaries have now averaged $135,403 a year. “The salaries we have here are at the top,” said Bruce Clark, a Polk attorney. “They are fair, but we don’t see a lot of them here doing felony cases, just looking at some of the misdemeanor cases.” Two different counties Grimes County is home to more than 10 percent of the state’s felony defendants, but only one felony trial court judge works there. She is in Houston. From 2017-2018, Grayson County has seen a nearly $2 million reduction in funding for felony trials, due in large part to interventions, according to reports provided by officials and an analysis of publicly available records. Including the $1 million in funding cuts related to those cases either in diversion or at trial, the overall number of cases accepted into diversion has dropped by 35 percent, while the number of trials has dropped by 27 percent, according to a report prepared in 2015 and provided to ProPublica. Grayson County judges raised the trial judge salary to $93,621 a year in 2017, well above the statewide salary of $60,004. To put that in perspective, the median base pay for a current district judge in Grayson County is just more than $71,000

Although I believe that there is a strong positive signal in bioluminescence, I think that signal is far from clear in every case, and scientists should therefore proceed with caution until there is enough data to form a stronger conclusion. In terms of cause and effect, I find that there is no good reason to prioritize universality over heterogeneous effects. Intersectionality is probably a stronger choice because it is only relevant when there is reason to believe that different organisms are affected by a particular effect. On the other hand, determination of cause and effect is an ambiguous concept, in which there is no way to steer the direction toward simplicity or complexity. However, in many cases, scientists can assign a value to the scale for significance or complexity. The result need not be a perfect ratio to serve the purpose of suggesting a particular cause. For example, the story for the complexity effect is much more complicated than it is for diversity and complexity effects (a point noted by Richard Harrelson). In spite of the small body of evidence, I believe that scientists and policymakers alike must continue to work toward a consensus about the value of the bioluminescence effect, by focusing efforts in pursuit of scientific evidence. To achieve this goal, we must value the role of science in informing public policy, but also be open to the ways in which the bioluminescence effect can be used to benefit the economy and improve livelihoods. to relay the dialogue in a different way after having worked alongside Final Fantasy Voice Actor Messners. It’s a little bit jarring to not get much of words, as sometimes the main characters will complete a conversation without saying a word outside of the raptorical situations provided by the dialog. While the dialogue itself has issues, the design and map are absolutely beautiful throughout the game. The game allows players to explore certain areas and hidden areas providing players with clues to the story. This is something that has already happened in the story segment, as well as something that continues throughout the game. Towns are detailed and have recognizable features, such as churches in Gan Altula and church bells being placed in the ground in many towns throughout the game. The art design and visual design really come together in a way that has not been seen before. The layout of towns is also a nod to the locations Final Fantasy XI is set, such as people eating brunch at cafés, coffee shops, and winding roadways. One feature not seen outside of most Final Fantasy games, is the inclusion of a timed clock on dungeon entrances (a nod to Dragon Quest). This is a noticeable feature of the plot and helps keep players from being lost where their character is unlike in Tales of Zestiria. It also helps let players access a map and see the hour and day value in each spot. Collectibles and Achievements The progression of gems takes up a lot of the game and it ties into the overall gameplay very well. Acquiring gems is done by fighting against enemies, while some side quests are also a necessity to finish as these quests require gems to continue progressing (such as the dungeon diver). Slaying the monsters is another way of obtaining gems, but the ability to obtain additional gems from nearby monsters is also important as you only have a limited amount to use for each monster. Potions are also a good way to carry over gems which allow you to quickly fill your inventory by using limited items obtained from enemies, as you also get rewarding for keeping items stored in your inventory (much like Tales of Zestiria does). Leveling up some of the minor characters is a good way to earn additional gems, but additional potions are also given out for completed dungeons as special rewards for advanced players. While the game’s difficulty pressures the player to focus on exploration over combat, the gem system has a strong bonus focus for gaining resources and abilities for your character. There are several ways to gain stat boosts, but there are also options that can enhance your character further by obtaining bullets from nearby monsters. The story segment is relatively well paced and allows players to easily move through the lengthy plot of the Chrono King Noctis and Queen Azazel. Several new plot points are introduced throughout the plot, including unlocking new dungeons and creating scenarios that will be revisited by players in search of new experiences. Much like Tales of Zestiria does, there are several ways to earn rewards for completing dungeons, but there are some secret side quests like capturing monsters and collecting crystals from dungeons. Monster slaying and defeating common enemies is touched upon in the storyline, but there are also stronger monsters that will appear in random events (like you often see in SEGA games). Standard slaying quests bring out regular monsters and also challenge quests that involve taking out monsters with a more rare chance of appearing. There is also a diary system that allows the player to keep track of how much of the game went in various areas, such as the notebook counts the amount of monsters you have taken out. While I doubt such calculators become widely available in the future, the recently released Tales of Zestiria offers something similar in this regard. Final Thoughts There is no doubt that the Final Fantasy XI RPG has a major impact on the series. Bringing captivating characters, great story, and competitive gameplay, it offers a different kind of RPG experience to that found in other Final Fantasy titles, while still offering enjoyable platforming gameplay in the traditional Final Fantasy style. For a title worked on by 3D Realms, the voice acting is superb and sets the tone for any Final Fantasy RPG. The story segment is far from perfect, but offers a great framework for some of the events found in Final Fantasy games in a different manner. A feature that should be noted is a soundtrack that can be downloaded from the game. The soundtrack overlooks the overall development of the story, and points out that the groundwork for future development has been set already with the introduction of Ulamis and will be set up further in later games. There’s also a very engaging questline that can be used to plan expeditions through locations, and the mission sequence shows the quests that will potentially continue the story based on choices seen in Final Fantasy VII Episode Duscae. This is a good way for players to see what else will be going on in the larger world sections in Serendia,

the Constitution’s Due Process Clause, which guarantees due process to detainees in jails and prisons. A wide assortment of immigrant rights groups seek to extend similar protections to immigrants subject to deportation or migration detention. Typically, Immigration and Customs Enforcement, Border Patrol, and other agencies subject to the executive executive are administrative agencies, which engage in enforcement through the levers of federal regulation and executive order but do not have the authority to prosecute crimes. These agencies, like all other federal agencies, are ultimately subordinate to the executive branch. But these agencies have long been largely protected from political interference and entrusted with enforcement of the laws they enforce. Immigration has been on the table in negotiations between the White House and congressional Democrats for months — in addition to political leaders like Trump, who has long invoked a crisis on the border to bolster his own political fortunes. While federal judges have ruled the president has the power to fix the situation on the border, and Supreme Court Justice Neil Gorsuch has suggested the courts should directly be involved in enforcing immigration restrictions, the judiciary has limited actual authority to regulatory agencies charged with enforcing the laws the executive branch is supposed to carry out with discretion. And the Trump administration has made broad calls to curb illegal immigration, for example by setting new vetting standards and limiting the number of asylum seekers allowed in the US. But the Republican administration itself doesn’t have much clout over prosecutions. Instead, Congress has granted the attorney general huge amounts of prosecutorial discretion, including the use of a mechanical discretion to determine whether a case it could agree to ends up in federal court. That has secreted out potentially criminal immigrants for deportation, and sometimes even migrants seeking to remain in the US during deportation. On Wednesday, House Democrats introduced a bill to expand this prosecutorial authority to cover just one category of crime: trafficking. But it would also make similar protections available to other groups, including lawyers who help unaccompanied minors seek immigration status and women who try to avoid being forced into prostitution. Party leaders sought to highlight their immigration reform proposals Wednesday at an event organized by Helena Gonzalez, an American citizen who was deported back to the United States after a Mexican court twice sentenced men to prison for exploiting her as a prostitute, among other offenses. The Democrats’ bills mirror a concern being loudly raised by branches of the Trump administration. The proposals would likely centralize the enforcement arms of the government, with the attorney general taking on more responsibility for enforcing the law, even if they don’t directly expand the resources he or she brings to bear. But congressional Republicans look likely to block the issue fiercely, much in the way they have with healthcare reform; even as some Democrats have voiced support for broadening the prosecutions the attorney general can bring, Republicans cut an notably skeptical tone. A lot more power, a lot less accountability Rep. Gerry Connolly (D-VA), who serves as the minority whip in the chamber, said the legislation would not require the attorney general to sign off on any additional prosecutions. “Trump is enforcing the law on his own,” he said. “It should allow him to take that case, see it through the system and send it to the trial judge without us being there to make the final decision.” His bill would remove that discretion, he added, but would allow for the attorney general to defer a prosecution in the meantime. Some of his colleagues were more skeptical of Republican’s position. “The attorney general already has enormous discretion over what particular cases should be brought today, which is simply to follow the direction of the law; I don’t anticipate that changing,” said Rep. Jan Schakowsky (D-IL). “We need to ensure our immigration laws are being enforced effectively and consistently, and to do so we need to do it in a way that only modestly expands the existing power of the executive branch and hardly diminishes it in the process.” Stephanie Comstock, chairman of the National Republican Senatorial Committee, said in a statement that the bill “dramatically expands every program run by the DoJ, including immigration enforcement and human trafficking. It will break the system, create loopholes designed for asylum seekers to come to the border on any visa they want and shield them from a felony.” These concerns appear unfounded at best. For example, the immigration law’s prosecution process, which is already overseen by the attorney general, hasn’t been hailed as a model for the treatment of trafficking or similar crimes. In fact, trafficking prosecutions have been left in recent years to a handful of dedicated prosecutors who devote their own resources and handle their own cases, including the federal attorneys involved in the Epstein sex trafficking case. The legislation wouldn’t have to address all these questions. But if the move were to result in the expansion of the immigration law, it would amount to a much broader reorganization of the Justice Department’s other investigative and prosecutorial responsibilities. proposal to avoid a precarious budget situation that arises in recent years when Americans end up spending more than they receive in federal revenues. The federal government cannot borrow money, but it can adopt a budget. The CBO put off the rush to develop the Democratic platform for months after Biden dropped out of the 2020 race to see how proposals would affect the nation's finances. But there are several principles that focused on budget should be put in place if former Vice President Biden is the nominee. Federal Tax Raises Bernie Sanders does not tax high-earning individuals To make the tax increases affecting the middle classes an equal burden, Sanders plans to eliminate the loopholes that now protect the top earners from paying taxes. For up to 45% of their earnings, taxpayers can choose to defer capital gains taxes. The biggest break in taxes benefits high-earning individuals because they often save so much of their future earnings that they pay into employer-sponsored retirement accounts. A higher-income worker with an effective income tax rate of 28% would face a tax increase of about $20 in additional wage payments after paying taxes for capital gains and dividends. Sanders wants to create a "proportional deduction" position by eliminating the personal deduction and excluding capital gains. Under the Senators proposal, taxpayers who say they worked full-time could choose to pay less in taxes while receiving a smaller tax bill, without having to put more money into the coffers. Biden’s tax proposal takes a more ambitious approach. It proposes a tax increase to the highest earners for incomes 46% and more. The CBO has estimated that many benefits of the proposal actually come from increased tax revenue. While Biden has called his tax proposal a jobs program, critics of the plan say its cost estimate is too extravagant. Health: Couldn’t get a firm grip on costs for this benefit. A $1,000 tax credit and rebates remain untouched. America has saved billions of dollars for treatments to remove kidney stones and protect against diabetes. The Committee for Responsible Federal Budget put the total savings from these cuts in medical spending at $91 billion a year. A recent report by the American Nurses Association has not set a broader coverage option in this vacuum. The CBO did not provide a cost estimate on its proposed universal health plan because its model does not currently include specialty treatments. Health Care: Will not sign enough uninsured Americans up for coverage Sanders’ overall plan would also expand Medicare coverage to about 32 million Americans, almost doubling the current number. The presidential candidate’s ambitious proposals are largely in line with administration estimates, adding more than $10 trillion in spending on national healthcare over the next decade. The CBO has not offered estimates to proposals that would bring health coverage to millions more Americans. Biden urging Congress to create a "universal" option for health care coverage could draw voters from Republicans, but independents high percentages of Americans support an option. Health Care: Will not make enough progress in providing affordable health care Medicare has been a basic coverage provision for most Americans since 1965. However, rising health care costs and public disillusionment with affordable health care have left behind many voters in the presidential election. Sanders’ proposal would eliminate private health insurance and establish a public option, but the plan goes even further beyond the nationwide trend to lower prices. The CBO does not yet provide estimated health care costs, but Sanders should use the estimate to understand how his Medicare-for-all would affect beneficiaries. Social Security: Assumes raising the age for benefits from 65 to 67 Though the Social Security benefits already reach 67, Sanders’ plan does not build finances around his call to raise the retirement age. The revenue that supports the basic funding of Social Security, the payroll tax, doesn’t vary a lot from generation to generation. Most Americans will begin paying taxes at 18 and start their benefits at age 55. Some people may then receive Social Security and get their full benefits before reaching age 65, and younger workers may get their benefits as soon as age 64. But the Social Security Trust Fund includes a mechanism to help lock in future growth of Social Security without changing the age. Social Security benefits and payments are now capped at the time of the person’s death, but if Sanders wants to extend Social Security, he should propose a mechanism to make it current. Tax Policy: Provides $2 trillion-plus savings under the Sanders's plan These savings would barely grow over the course of the next decade. Biden has relied heavily on the nonpartisan Tax Policy Center’s estimate of $2 trillion a year. Sanders’ tax plan covers the complex mechanisms for shifting revenue in the tax code. It is complicated and a precise figure depends on another detailed action taken. Sanders should start with calculating the budget impact of his plan on an annual basis to see whether it reduces the change. Emissions: Provides savings for most everyone in the near-term Emissions of many pollutants must be drastically reduced to address climate change. Biden proposes cutting industrial carbon emissions by implementing a cap-and-trade program.

Hawaii is already feeling the impact of the deadly Zika virus. Reports of Zika cases began earlier this month in central and western parts of the Big Island. In other counties like Kahakulai and Kauai, reports of Zika cases have been confirmed by positive lab results. Many of the cases are being attributed to mosquitoes. Honolulu’s Discovery Island Medical Center issued a health notice Tuesday warning that anyone in the community could be exposed to Zika virus. While the risk continues to be low, it urged people who may have been exposed to report to a health department or health care provider for possible Zika testing and treatment. “A potential carrier of the mosquito/virus combination might have occurred in unusually close proximity to the exposed person,” the clinic said in a statement. The U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention said human cases of Zika virus infection from mosquitoes are rare. A person infected with Zika virus that comes from a mosquito can develop anaphylaxis (breathing difficulties), reddening of the lymph nodes, and a “sporadic complication of conjunctivitis/symaphsis and coughing or wheezing,” according to the federal health agency. Only Hawaii County on the east side and Kalanapali County on the north side have currently reported no positive lab tests for the virus. The Fighting Island hospital encourages anyone concerned about Zika to come to one of its clinics for testing and consultation with their doctor. The CDC has activated the Prevention Health Outreach Helpline. On other islands like Maui, medical professionals and physicians are diagnosing cases of Zika virus in neighborhoods like Lahaina and Kamehameha. The most advanced of those patients are being treated at the Maui Medical Center. In Waimanalo, residents are rushing to the Waianae County Health Department to get their vaccines. Such concerns have prompted public health officials to reach out to state officials for special exceptions to address any potential obstacles Big Island residents face to getting their vaccinations and how serious. Waianae Health Director Susan Pacagni said she would consider an exception for influenza vaccines. People will probably not get their vaccine, she said, if they have the virus or they are infected by someone who shows signs of being infected. Waianae officials held a community presentation about the virus at 6:30 p.m. Monday night at the Waimanalo Senior Living Center, a regional senior care center in Waianae. If Neptune is capable of supporting life, a lander will be a key marker for episodic exploration. Deep data collected by a exploration mission will be essential to unravel the deep mysteries of the solar system for future research and exploration. Neptune is not just a moon but also a partner planet alongside the Earth. Such a close association has its own complexity of chemistry, gravitational coupling, and exchange of resources. It also has the reputation of possessing among the most dangerous rocks in the solar system: pyromolites. Then, of course, there is the presence of an extra-terrestrial visitor. This makes it important that landers be sent to the surface to better illuminate its properties and establish a safe presence for future human exploration missions into Deep Space. Another major priority of the Program is looking into Neptune’s surface landscape, its underlying geography (geophysics), and atmospheric materials that may have characteristics that could relate to extra-terrestrial biology analogous to what we already know from Neptune’s Region 4. The development of a lander is an important priority going forward. certificates, which can cause conflict for transitioning identities and lives. “What she intends to do with the document, that’s something that I think they will take into consideration as they move forward in their case,” Coburn said. Walters filed a motion to show the court should consider he and Watson the lawful registrors in the case. The judge ruled that Watson holds a birth certificate which apparently contradicts key elements of Watson’s filing, including name changes, birth date and the listing of stepfather. Watson’s mother, a retired mailman named Melanie Brock, sat at his side during the hearing. Melanie Brock said getting legally married was long a dream for her son. “Well, he was pregnant,” Melanie Brock said. “He wanted to get married and it was very complicated. He just took a nap and stopped worrying about it.” The 22-year-old man is training to be a nurse and has graduated from high school, but he still needs the stepfather for final college credit programs. They’re talking over a copy of the joint birth certificate, Melanie Brock said. Walters said the judge who heard Watson’s case needed to do more research before making the decision because she appeared to be appealing to in-state laws. And attorneys’ closing statements suggest that the lawyers themselves seemed focused on other cases in making the decision. “I don’t think that the guardian or sole parent is quite as important,” said the woman who filed as Watson’s guardian. “The adult can leave you and you are not responsible for procuring them. “Your life is a different phase, and you are free to do other things as you get older,” she continued. “Children grow up with you for careers and things like that. Caring for them in every way is still your role, except in a different way.” Coburn said the ruling is, indeed, a good thing for all legally married Colorado couples. But state officials have to be on their “peak behavior” when changing laws governing marriage recognition, he said, because they are constantly complaining about what other states are doing. “We do have a statewide marriage statute, but there is very little application of it. There’s a lot of conflicting interpretation of what the law is saying,” Coburn told DPA. “If I’m your child’s guardian and I support what you want to do, at least try to comply with the statute, because you may feel that’s unfair.” Morgan Goldbloom, president of Transgender Rights Colorado, agreed, saying state officials should work to reduce public confusion about laws in other states. Every time trans people make a move to expand recognition, the uproars over filing for joint birth certificates are the same, Goldbloom said. “Anytime you want to change the law on this issue, it’s a subject of very heated scrutiny from your constituents back home,” she said. Obtaining marriage certificates under plural relationships can be very complicated. Dozens of Utah legally married couples interviewed by DPA reported having trouble establishing their legal names, but listed their relationships, which were later picked up by government databases and then recorded in state databases for all couples seeking marriage certificates. In Colorado, many longtime partners have taken a different approach, filing subjudital marriage documents on a separate form with a relationship and legal name, again for state records only. The policy, which has been in effect for two decades, creates clarity for Colorado officials and separates joint partners — like Goldbloom, who just met her wife this summer — from other marriages. For many, though, such separation is clouded by the possibility their legal name on the state document could violate their privacy. Beth Denning of Berthoud, Colorado, says she changed her name to “Clementyn” on her marriage document because she’d like to have state officials know she’s a plural relationship for privacy reasons. Also, just because the married document says she’s married to her best friend, not a partner, doesn’t mean her fellow citizens need to know she loves them. She faces a legal fight with the ACLU since the state’s marriage licensing division has refused to issue her a marriage certificate with any reference to her true legal name. She has a new partner and legal advisor who’s socially and politically savvy and believes they’ll be able to wait out the fight. “He was state legislature officer, and he’s obviously read tons of books and done a huge amount of research on the political scene, so he knows where exactly public opinion stands vis-à-vis transgender people,” she said. “You know, I’m not with anybody … and if you’re with anything, death knows all.” Denning regigned her relationship and filed to include her date

: How bodies are shifting There were around 15,000 people thought to be in more temporary shelters, he added - a figure not yet confirmed by the Nepalese authorities. Army commander Rajendra Prakash said about 500 soldiers were being brought in to help and many others deployed from other parts of Nepal. He told BBC News that they had set up four women's base camps and were giving out mats and tents and asking Indian soldiers to help with hauling. The army has also erected a number of tents across the country. Video caption Nepal quake: Villagers describe fighting to keep homes intact Mr Prakash told the BBC he hoped the tents would be the centre of a "long-scale" operation to help people living at makeshift shelters. He also commended public awareness and said the army and local authorities were already providing aid to those affected by the disaster. A statement from the Federation of Red Crescent Societies in Nepal called for urgent humanitarian action across the country. The rough areas have no permanent shelter facilities, tents, clinics, medical teams or tents. "Everything that is essential to rebuild can be provided at very short notice," the statement added. 'People are dying' Buses and aid deliveries were proving crucial initially, said Prabhat Dorjee, an engineer in Nepal's capital, Kathmandu. "People were arriving without shelter, without food, without water," he said, speaking at a shelter in the city. When he found the building where he works, a storage unit, had been badly damaged, he was given no other choice, he said. "It's also a government property, they told the tenants their houses would be destroyed if they tried to move out of town. Where were we going to go?" Image copyright AFP/Getty Images Image caption Sita Bhal and her friend reached a government shelter Sita Bhal has Alzheimer's disease and had not been given a life jacket. She said it had taken three or four hours to reach a government shelter because the devastation was too bad to navigate the roads. "We are trying to feed them but they are scared to death," she told the BBC, standing outside a branch of the Keeber Gang charity in the Nepalese capital, Vashi Matang. Then, she said, members of the humanitarian collective, which is currently hosting 317 people, handed over food and cooking supplies. Children had boiled tea and boiled chicken bones, taping up the wet remnants with tea towels to eat for the night. Ludhana Dorja, a 55-year-old teacher in Kathmandu, was among those staying at the local government shelter. "I didn't even dare to go outside with my colleagues because we were afraid. There were so many hurt people - kids and people seriously injured," she told the BBC. "[Some people] were so wet that they didn't have towels… and grabbed any foil they could find, and put the bucket in their mouth. It was just so cold. People are dying, people are hungry." Indications of what happens next are still looking hazy. Authorities have acknowledged that the earthquake is a disaster that requires major humanitarian assistance. But they said they also needed to develop an immediate post-impact strategy in a bid to rebuild the affected areas. Local police chief Lt General Uttendra Phul said at least 24,000 soldiers and police had been deployed and nearly 4,000 personnel had been deployed. Corface roads had been opened and temporary shelters were being built by relief workers, police said. The 2015 earthquake that struck with a magnitude of 7.8 in southern Nepal caused tens of thousands of deaths, destroyed villages, triggered landslides and halted infrastructure work across the region. "They were very shaken by what that earthquake did, but importantly, they recovered from it. That is why they are so strong now," said Dr David Pizzolosi, a British doctor recently returned from a medical stint in the affected region. But the latest disaster, which has killed 558 people, has been far more difficult to contain.
